local_names.romanized_local: Jian Dan Juan (Chinese); Telur Dadar Goreng (Malay)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fried egg omelette as a prepared dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving is one fried egg omelette made from approximately two eggs, weighing around 100 g.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Egg Omelette — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Fried Egg Omelette (cr_id G00010607). Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, sodium, dietary fibre, sugar) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central entry for fried egg (FDC ID 172185) as the closest approved-source equivalent, with adaptation noted. The HPB-reported fat_g of 0.0 is anomalous for a fried preparation; this value is preserved as reported by the primary source and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — egg-based dish with negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Fried egg omelette contains only 2.36 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign GI values to foods with negligible carbohydrate content. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0. Per Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods, gi_category is set to not_applicable and glycemic_index is set to the numeric sentinel value 0 to satisfy template numeric-type requirement while gi_category documents the not_applicable status.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~180 mg/100 g) with no added sugar or high saturated fat
hypertension.guidance: A plain fried egg omelette is low in sodium and suitable for hypertensive individuals when prepared with minimal added salt and unsaturated oil; avoid adding soy sauce, fish sauce, or table salt to keep sodium intake controlled.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.36 g/100 g) and high protein (10.7 g/100 g) with negligible glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: The fried egg omelette has negligible carbohydrate content and a high protein-to-carbohydrate ratio, making it an excellent low-glycaemic choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; pair with non-starchy vegetables and avoid high-GI accompaniments such as white bread or sweetened sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate dietary cholesterol (~373 mg/100 g) from egg yolk; fat_g reported as 0.0 by HPB source which is anomalous
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Egg consumption of up to one egg per day is generally considered acceptable within a heart-healthy diet for most individuals; prepare using unsaturated oil (canola or olive oil), avoid butter or lard, and limit to 4–6 eggs per week if LDL cholesterol is elevated.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Egg Omelette (煎蛋卷; Telur Dadar Goreng) is a simple, widely consumed egg-based dish in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It is prepared by beating one or more whole eggs, seasoning lightly with salt, and pan-frying in a small amount of cooking oil until the egg is set and lightly golden on the outside while remaining tender inside. The texture is soft and yielding with a slightly crisp edge, and the flavour is mild, savoury, and eggy. In Singapore, it is commonly served as a side dish at hawker centres alongside rice or porridge, or as a quick protein component in a mixed-plate meal. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (10.7 g/100 g) and very low in carbohydrates (2.36 g/100 g), making it an excellent low-glycaemic protein source suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or following a low-carbohydrate diet. The HPB-reported fat value of 0.0 g/100 g is anomalous for a fried preparation and likely reflects a data entry limitation; actual fat content from frying oil is expected to be present. Eggs are a rich source of vitamin B12, vitamin D, choline, selenium, and dietary cholesterol. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-friendly when prepared without pork-derived ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a fried egg omelette?
Is fried egg omelette good for diabetics?
Can I eat fried egg omelette if I have high blood pressure?
Is fried egg omelette high in cholesterol?
How much protein in a fried egg omelette?
Is fried egg omelette gluten free?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo canonical entry for Fried Egg Omelette (cr_id G00010607, confidence 0.95). Secondary nutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central fried egg entry (FDC ID 172185) as the closest approved-source equivalent. The anomalous fat_g value of 0.0 is preserved as reported and documented.
notes: fat_g is reported as 0.0 by the HPB Singapore Foodo primary source. This value is anomalous for a fried egg preparation, which typically contains 8–12 g fat per 100 g from both the egg yolk and frying oil. The 0.0 value is preserved as reported by the approved primary source (HPB) and is confirmed zero per HPB source data; however, users should be aware this likely reflects a data limitation rather than a true zero fat content. saturated_fat_g is set to 0.0 consistent with the HPB-reported fat_g of 0.0; confirmed zero per HPB source data. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as eggs contain no dietary fibre — naturally zero. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1.0: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific HPB fried egg omelette entry is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central fried egg entry at the level of individual fatty acid fractions as of 2026-04-16; source consulted: USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 172185. Secondary nutrients (sugar_g, sodium_mg, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamins, minerals, water_g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 172185 (Egg, whole, cooked, fried) as the closest approved-source equivalent; adaptation documented in nutrient_source. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (2.36 g/100 g); gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. The numeric field glycemic_index is set to 0 as a sentinel to satisfy JSON numeric-type schema requirements; the not_applicable status is authoritatively recorded in gi_category and gi_source.

veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Fried Egg Omelette (G00010607) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8. The q4 failure from iteration 2 (glycemic_index must be numeric) is resolved by setting glycemic_index to the numeric sentinel value 0 while gi_category=not_applicable and gi_source clearly document that GI is not applicable for this negligible-carbohydrate food. Core macros are sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (primary approved source). The anomalous fat_g=0.0 value is preserved as reported and fully documented. Secondary nutrients are adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 172185. All Singaporean mandatory multilingual names present. Aliases consistency check passed. Ontology anchors defensible. Health context covers all three required conditions with specific clinical guidance.
